coredata.dc:title: One-pot synthesis of aqueous soluble and organic soluble carbon dots and their multi-functional applications
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Junfen
Li, Pengxia
Wang, Dongxiu
Dong, Chuan
coredata.dc:description: Two kinds of carbon dots (CDs) with different solubility, aqueous soluble CDs (ACDs) and organic soluble CDs (OCDs), were produced at the same time by one step hydrothermal synthesis method using sorbic acid and proline as carbon precursor and nitrogen dopant, respectively. The synthesized CDs were characterized by means of UV, fluorescence, TEM, elemental analysis, IR and XPS et al. Based on the effective and proportional fluorescence quenching by Cr(VI), ACDs were successfully used as a high sensitive and selective probe for Cr(VI) detection. Internal filtration effect (IFE) played an important role in the quenching process. Under the optimal conditions, linear response for Cr(Ⅵ) was observed in the range of 0.5–100 μmol/L, and the calculated detection limit was 34 nmol/L. The method has been satisfactorily applied to detect Cr(VI) ions in real water samples of our campus. Then, the ACDs were further applied for cell imaging in B16F10 cells. Furthermore, OCDs were well dispersed into PMMA to fabricate OCDs/PMMA composites as fluorescent films.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Organic soluble CDs
Aqueous soluble CDs
Fluorescence
Cr (VI) detection
Bioimaging
Fluorescent films

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescent sensing of Cr(VI) with ACDs
Preparation of the fluorescent OCDs/PMMA composite films
Characterizations of the ACDs and OCDs
Optical properties of the ACDs and OCDs
Fluorescent sensing of Cr(VI) with ACDs
Toxicity of the ACDs and bioimaging

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Shanxi Province
National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images (the scale is 50 nm and 20 nm) of the (A, B)ACDs and (C, D)OCDs. Inset: size distribution histograms of the (A)ACDs and (C) OCDs; HRTEM images of an individual (B)ACD and (D)OCD.
FT-IR spectra of the ACDs and OCDs.
(A,E)XPS full survey, (B,F)C1S, (C,G)N1S and (D,H)O1S spectra of the (A-D)ACDs and (E-H)OCDs.

(A)UV–vis absorption spectra(CACDs = 0.30 mg/mL, COCDs = 0.20 mg/mL), (B)the maximum excitation and emission spectra of the ACDs in water and OCDs in ethanol (CACDs = 0.25 mg/mL, COCDs = 0.80 mg/mL). Inset: photographs of the ACDs in water (left) and OCDs in ethanol (right) under 365 nm UV light.

(A)Fluorescence emission spectra of the ACDs against different concentrations of Cr(VI)(0.5, 1.0, 5.0, 10, 20, 40, 60, 80, 100, 120, 140, 160, 180, 200, 300, 400, 500, 600 and 700 μmol/L); (B) the Stern−Volmer (SV) plot of the changes of fluorescent intensities of the ACDs with increasing Cr(VI) concentration(0.5–100 μmol/L); (C) the UV–vis absorption spectra of different ions; (D) fluorescence decay of the ACDs without and with Cr(VI) at λex/λem of 405/430 nm. CACDs = 0.25 mg/mL.

(A)Comparison of fluorescence intensity of the ACDs in the presence of various ions (0.33 mmol/L); (B) Comparison of fluorescence intensity of the ACDs after the addition of Cr(VI) (0.33 mmol/L) and other different ions (1.65 mmol/L). CACDs = 0.25 mg/mL.
Laser scanning confocal microscopy images of B16F10 cells incubating without ACDs as a control and with ACDs for 1 h. CACDs = 0.45 mg/mL.
Comparison of different methods for Cr(VI) detection.
Determination of Cr(VI) in real water samples (n = 6).

head.ce:title: One-pot synthesis of aqueous soluble and organic soluble carbon dots and their multi-functional applications

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan, 030006, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Chemistry, Environmental-science and Engineering-study Center, Shanxi University, Taiyuan, 030006, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Chemistry, Environmental-science and Engineering-study Center, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Two kinds of carbon dots (CDs) with different solubility, aqueous soluble CDs (ACDs) and organic soluble CDs (OCDs), were produced at the same time by one step hydrothermal synthesis method using sorbic acid and proline as carbon precursor and nitrogen dopant, respectively. The synthesized CDs were characterized by means of UV, fluorescence, TEM, elemental analysis, IR and XPS et al. Based on the effective and proportional fluorescence quenching by Cr(VI), ACDs were successfully used as a high sensitive and selective probe for Cr(VI) detection. Internal filtration effect (IFE) played an important role in the quenching process. Under the optimal conditions, linear response for Cr(Ⅵ) was observed in the range of 0.5–100 μmol/L, and the calculated detection limit was 34 nmol/L. The method has been satisfactorily applied to detect Cr(VI) ions in real water samples of our campus. Then, the ACDs were further applied for cell imaging in B16F10 cells. Furthermore, OCDs were well dispersed into PMMA to fabricate OCDs/PMMA composites as fluorescent films.

ACDs and OCDs were produced at the same time and their multi-functional applications were explored like Cr(Ⅵ) detection, bioimaging, and OCDs/PMMA films.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Luminescent carbon nanodots (CDs) have drawn great attentions owing to their advantages of small particle size, favorable photostability, good biocompatibility, and low toxicity [1]. There are two kinds of CDs appeared in recent researches, aqueous soluble CDs (ACDs) and organic soluble CDs (OCDs). Usually, ACDs can be easily obtained by hydrothermal, microwave treatment, pyrolysis, electrochemical oxidation et al. [2]. The hydrothermal method has been the most widely used because of its simplicity in large-scale synthesis. ACDs with good solubility in water are widely researched in the domains of chemical and biological probes, drug and gene delivery, and bioimaging system. In contrast, OCDs are only dispersed in organic solvents and rarely researched [3]. For example, Bourlinos et al. reported the synthesis of OCDs from lauryl gallate in CHCl3 [4]. In Panniello's work, OCDs were synthesized by citric acid in the presence of hexadecylamine in high boiling organic solvent [5]. However, it is a remarkable fact that OCDs can be used as phosphors and encapsulation materials for photovoltaic devices, such as LED [6]. The synthesis method, emission mechanism and application of OCDs are still needed to be developed. To the best of our knowledge, the fabrication of ACDs and OCDs at the same time from one step synthesis has only been reported by Gu and co-workers [7] by using the synthesized waterborne polyurethane (WPU) as raw material. ACDs were used in bioimmaging and OCDs were suitable for preparing fluorescent composite films with polymers.

Cr (VI) is well known as a pollutant to the environment and is highly harmful to human health. It causes serious diseases such as gastrointestinal reactions, kidney damage, nasal septal damage, and even deadly organic cancers [8,9]. Hence, the discharge of Cr(VI) in environmental water must be strictly controlled. According to the World Health Organization (WHO), the allowed maximum concentration of Cr(VI) in drinking water is 50 μg/L [10]. Monitoring the content of Cr(VI) ions accurately and fast in water to avoid excessive intake is a matter of great urgency. A great many of methods for the determination of Cr(VI) have been reported, including atomic absorption spectrometry, electrochemical analysis, ion chromatography, inductively coupled plasma mass spectrum, ultraviolet spectroscopy [11–13], and so on. These methods mainly rely on expansive equipment or complicated procedures. For example, determination of Cr(VI) by spectrophotometry must use diphenyl carbazide as chromogenic agent. Cr(VI) was reduced to Cr(III) after the reaction and formed fuchsia complex. Fluorescence method has now been widely used for analysis because of simple, high sensitivity and high selectivity. Since the boom of CDs, a number of fluorescent sensors for determination of Cr(VI) have been provided [14]. In addition, insufficient selectivity is often encountered when the sensors are applied in Cr(VI) detection. It should also be noted that the sensitivity of this method depends on the luminescent property of the sensors which must possess high fluorescence quantum yield. Therefore, it is still significant to find simple and green fluorescent CDs sensors that have high sensitivity and selectivity in aqueous systems, and further improve their PL properties.

Many raw materials are ideally suitable precursors to produce CDs. For example, fruit acids have been reported as carbon materials for the synthesis of CDs [15,16]. In addition, doping with other elements such as N, P, S, and B, especially N, the compositions, structures, spectral properties and applications of CDs could be effectively adjusted and improved. Due to nontoxicity and abundant amino groups, amino acids are very suitable to synthesize N doped CDs with superior properties [17]. In this article, we select l-proline and Sorbic Acid as nitrogen and carbon source, respectively. What deserves to be mentioned the most is that l-proline and sorbic acid in this paper are commercially available and could be used to synthesize ACDs and OCDs directly. Compared with the WPU emulsion in Ref. [7], complex synthesis of starting materials was not needed and the synthesis procedure was easier.

Herein, by one step hydrothermal synthesis, blue fluorescent nitrogen-doped ACDs and OCDs were obtained at the same time through simple separation and purification. Based on fluorescence quenching of the ACDs, the method of detecting trace Cr(VI) was established in phosphate buffer solution. Afterwards, ACDs were successfully applied as a staining probe for the bioimaging of B16F10 cells. We further fabricated the OCDs/PMMA composite films by dissolving OCDs into the polymethyl methacrylate (PMMA), which can be potential used as novel photovoltaic materials.

Sorbic Acid, l-proline, and polymethyl methacrylate (PMMA) were obtained from Aladdin. Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium (DMEM), penicillin, streptomycin, fetal bovine serum (FBS), Dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), 3-(4,5-Dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) were purchased from Solarbio (Beijing, China). All other reagents were analytical grade. Phosphate buffered solution (10 mmol/L) was prepared according to reference [1]. Double distilled water was used throughout the experiments.

Transmission electron microscopic (TEM) and high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images were obtained by JEM-2100 electron microscope (Tokyo, Japan). The particle size in solution and apparent zeta potential value were measured by dynamic light scattering (DLS) on a Zetasizer Nano ZS90 (Malvern Instruments Ltd., UK). The elemental analysis (C, H, and N) was carried out on an Elementar Analysensysteme vario EL cube elemental analyzer (Hanau, Germany). Fourier Transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum was recorded with a Thermo Scientific Nicolet iS50 Infrared Spectrometer (Thermo Corporation, USA). The elemental composition was recorded by an Axis Ultra Dld X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, AXIS ULTRA DLD, Britain). The UV–vis absorption spectra were acquired on an UV-265 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan). The fluorescence spectra of CDs were carried out on a F-4500 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Tokyo, Japan). 1 cm path-length quartz cell was used. Fluorescence lifetime was recorded by the FLS920(Edinburgh Instruments, England) with a 405 nm laser as the excitation source. Fluorescence images were collected with LSM880+airyscan confocal laser scanning microscope (Zeiss, Germany), and the ACDs were excited at 405 nm. The pH values of the solutions were measured by a pHS-3C pH meter (METTLER TOLEDO, Switzerland).

Sorbic acid and l-proline were selected as precursors to form ACDs and OCDs. Firstly, Sorbic acid (0.7607 g, 5 mmol) and l-proline (Pro) (0.5757g，5 mmol) was dissolved in 15 mL of double distilled water. 3 mins ultrasonic treatment was necessary to disperse the materials evenly. Then the mixture was put into a 20 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 5 h. The obtained white emulsion was centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 10 min and two obvious layers appeared. The upper layer was colorless aqueous solution and was named ACDs, while the under layer was oily yellow precipitate and named OCDs. ACDs were purified by filtering through a 0.22 μm microporous membrane and obtained by freeze-dried. The oily yellow precipitate was washed with water and dispersed into ethanol. Then, brown OCDs powder could be obtained after vacuum drying.

The quantum yield (QY) of CDs was measured using quinine sulfate (QY = 0.54) in 0.10 mol/L H2SO4 (refractive index, n = 1.33) as the standard based on the following equation [18]:
Y u = Y s ×
F u
F s
×
A s
A u
×
(
n u
n s
)
2
where Fs and Fu referred to the integrated emission intensity of quinine sulfate and CDs, respectively. As and Au were absorbance of the standard and CDs at the excitation wavelength, respectively. n was the solvent refractive index. ACDs were dissolved in double distilled water (n = 1.33) and OCDs were dissolved in ethanol (n = 1.36), respectively. The fluorescence spectra were recorded under the excitation of 350 nm for ACDs and 365 nm for OCDs, respectively. Absorbance of the two solutions was kept less than 0.05.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent sensing of Cr(VI) with ACDs
ce:section.ce:para: ACDs storage solution (15.00 mg/mL) was prepared by dissolving the solid powder in double distilled water and stored in refrigerator at 4 °C. In the fluorescence quenching procedure, 50 μL of the ACDs storage solution was added into 3.0 mL of PBS (10 mmol/L, pH 7.4), then different amounts of Cr2O7 2− solution was added to obtain various concentrations. The fluorescence emission spectra were scanned under the excitation at 350 nm, and the excitation/emission slits were set to 10 nm/10 nm. All experiments were performed at room temperature.

A symbolic MTT assay method was used in this paper to study the cell viability experiment of ACDs according to reference [19]. The density of B16F10 cells obtained was 5 × 104 cells/mL. Seeded 100 μL of B16F10 cells in each 96-well plate and incubated at 37 °C for 24 h in a humidified 5% CO2 atmosphere. After removing the media, the cells were treated with increasing concentrations of the ACDs solution from 0 to 0.8 mg/mL, and were then grown as previous process for another 24 h. Further, the original media was replaced with fresh medium containing 20 μL 5.0 mg/mL of MTT, and continually incubated for 4 h. Then, the MTT medium was exchanged with 100 μL of DMSO. After shaking for 15 min, the absorbance of the resulting mixture was measured at 570 nm using a microplate reader (Varioskan1 Flash, Thermo Electron Corporation). The 96-well control plate containing only cells was used in the relative cell viability experiment for comparison.

At the same conditions, mouse melanoma (B16F10) cells were cultured in the mixture of DMEM, penicillin (100 unit/mL), streptomycin (100 g/mL) and FBS (10%) and incubated for 24 h. Appropriate volume ACDs solution (15.00 mg/mL) was added to the above medium (1.00 mL) to make a 0.45 mg/mL final concentration. After an incubation of 1.0 h, B16F10 cells were harvested using 0.25% trypsin/0.020% EDTA, washed thoroughly three times with pH 7.4 PBS (1 mL each time) and kept in PBS for the fluorescence imaging.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the fluorescent OCDs/PMMA composite films
ce:section.ce:para: A certain amount of OCDs and PMMA were dissolved in dichloromethane and stirred thoroughly to form homogeneous mixture. The weight ratios of OCDs and PMMA were set as 0, 1, 2, and 3.0%. Then, the mixture was put into the glass plates. After 12 h curing at room temperature, the blue emissive OCDs/PMMA composite film was obtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterizations of the ACDs and OCDs
ce:section.ce:para: TEM was used for the structural properties of CDs, such as morphology and size distribution information. It was clear to see from the TEM image that both ACDs and OCDs were quasi spherical with perfect monodispersity (Fig. 1A∼D). The inset images in Fig. 1 B revealed that lattice spacing of ACDs was 0.24 nm, which indicated that the crystalline properties of ACDs were similar to the d spacings of the graphene (100) planes [20]. In the other words, nanocrystalline core of ACDs was predominantly sp2 carbon. However, the OCDs had no highly crystalline carbon structure (insets in Fig. 1D) [21]. The speculated reason was that sp2 carbons were destroyed during the formation procedure. The average size of the ACDs (by counting 100 particles) and OCDs (by counting 50 particles) were (1.7 ± 0.1) nm and (2.1 ± 0.1) nm respectively, which meant that the size of OCDs was larger than ACDs (inset images in Fig. 1A and C). The DLS result revealed that the average size of the ACDs in water and OCDs in ethanol were 3.39 nm and 21.92 nm respectively (Fig. S1), suggesting that the as-prepared CDs were stable in the above-mentioned solution without obvious aggregation.

The elemental composition of both CDs was investigated by the elemental analysis and the results were listed out. Table 1S revealed that both of them had similar elemental contents of C, H and O. However, the nitrogen component of ACDs was 9% higher than OCDs. It was estimated that most proline molecules joined the synthesis of ACDs during the hydrothermal process which led to the fewer nitrogen atoms on the surface of OCDs.

The precise information of chemical bonds and functional groups on the surface of both CDs were characterize by Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy. Fig. 2 showed the FTIR spectra of CDs which demonstrated that both ACDs and OCDs contained similar functional groups such as O–H or N–H (3000–3500 cm−1), –CH3 (2970, 2869 cm−1), –CH2 (1447 cm−1), C–NH (1368 cm−1) [22,23]. From this figure, we could also see that ACDs had more stronger C–NH absorption peaks than OCDs, which indicated more amine functional groups and higher nitrogen content of the ACDs. The peak at 1587 cm−1 implied the presence of CC groups [24]. The three weak bands at 1752, 1162 and 1042 cm−1 may be attributed to the stretching vibrations of CO, bending vibration and stretching vibration of C–OH in the carboxy group [25,26], which was favored to give ACDs good hydrophilic properties. For the spectrum of OCDs, the strong absorption peaks at 1695, 1264 and 1207 cm−1 corresponded to stretching vibrations of CO, asymmetric stretching vibration and symmetric stretching vibration of C–O–C in the ester bond [27] respectively, which displayed the presence of hydrophobic groups. Hense, the above results proved that the different solubility between ACDs and OCDs was caused by the property of groups existing on their surfaces.

To characterize the surface element composition of ACDs and OCDs, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed and the scan XPS spectra were shown in Fig. 3 . Peaks at ca. 531, 400 and 285 eV were separately correspond to O1s, N1s and C1s (Fig. 3A, E). The proportions of C, N, and O were listed out (Table 2S), which were similar to the results of element analysis (Table 1S). Four main components, CC, C–N, C–O and CO, were observed in the C1s XPS spectrum of the ACDs at the positions of about 284.7, 285.3, 286.2, and 288.1 eV respectively (Fig. 3B) [15]. The peaks of the N1s XPS spectrum of the ACDs around 400.3 eV and 401.1 eV were pointed to the nitrogen atom of C–N and N–H groups, respectively (Fig. 3C) [28]. The O1s XPS spectrum of the ACDs was composed of three peaks at ca. 530.6 eV, ca. 531.5 eV and ca. 533.0 eV, indicating the presence of –OH, *OC–O, and OC–O*(Fig. 3D) [29]. The presence of functional groups (amine, oxhydryl and carboxylic groups, etc) on the ACDs surface, which were consistent with FTIR analysis, imparted excellent solubility in water. In addition, peaks around 284.8, 285.5, and 288.9 eV of the C1s XPS spectrum of OCDs pointed to the CC, C–N and C-OOR groups, respectively (Fig. 3F) [10,30]. The N 1s spectrum of OCDs displayed two peaks at 399.8 eV (pyrrolic N) and 401.4 eV (N–H) (Fig. 3G) [24]. The O1s XPS spectrum of the OCDs was composed of two peaks at ca. 532.1 and ca. 533.5 eV, indicating the presence of C–OH, and OC–O*(Fig. 3H) [31]. These XPS analysis results, along with the FT-IR spectra, indicated that oxhydryl groups and ester bonds existed on the surface of the obtained OCDs. Furthermore, the Zeta potential values of ACDs and OCDs were also measured as −11.0 mV and −2.11 mV (Fig. S2). Vast oxhydryl and carboxylic groups on the ACDs’ surface were the possible reason for the lower potential of ACDs.

Based on the above conclusions, we speculated the formation process of the ACDs and OCDs. In the environment with high temperature and pressure, there were two reaction routes during the synthesis procedure. The first one was the amidation reaction between NH- of l-proline and COOH- of sorbic acid [32]. Then, sp2 carbon corn was formed by polymerization, and after carbonization, ACDs were obtained [33]. The second one was the nucleophilic addition reaction between NH- of l-proline and C2H4- of sobic acid [34]. In this reaction, diene was destroyed and disturbed carbon corn was formed after polymerization. In addition, esterification should also take place between the disturbed corn and the two starting materials. Finally, OCDs were obtained after carbonization. The obtained CDs contained hydrophilic chain (such as carboxy group) or hydrophobic chain (such as ester bond) for ACDs and OCDs separately.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of the ACDs and OCDs
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in the inset photographs of Fig. 4 , ACDs and OCDs showed similar blue color in water and ethanol respectively when excited with 365 nm UV light. They had strong absorption peaks at approximately 255 nm, which was attributed to the π-π* transition of aromatic sp2 band(Fig. 4A) [35].

Fig. 4B displayed maximum fluorescence wavelengths of 430 nm and 460 nm for ACDs and OCDs, when excited at 350 nm and 365 nm respectively. As shown in Fig. S3A, changing excitation wavelength of the ACDs from 300 to 410 nm, the emission peak red-shifted from 415 to 488 nm. As well, as shown in Fig. S3B, with increasing excitation wavelength of OCDs from 310 to 400 nm, the emission peak red shifted from 420 to 490 nm. That was to say that both ACDs and OCDs exhibited typical excitation-dependent photoluminescent behaviors, which may be resulted from the different sizes and varying emission sites on the surface of this two kinds of CDs [16]. Furthermore, using quinine sulfate as the reference, the quantum yield (QY) of ACDs in water and OCDs in ethanol were measured as 8.47% and 4.54%, respectively. The QY of ACDs was not very high, while the QY of our OCDs was comparable to that of reported [7]. As another literature explained, the doping N elements on the CDs could lead to the formation of a structure loaded with a lot of defects [36]. Those energy traps in the intrinsic carbon core contribute the improvement of QY of CDs [37]. So, in our study, the higher nitrogen proportion of ACDs really played an important role in the super PL property.

It was found that ACDs were well dissolved in water and kept stable for over six months (Fig. S4A). As displayed in Fig. S4B, even in NaCl solution with concentration higher than 3 mol/L, the fluorescence intensity of ACDs did not change. Fig. S4C exhibited the effect of pH on the emission intensity of ACDs. In the range of pH 5.0–10.0, the PL intensity of the ACDs was strong and constant, and decreased when the pH higher than 10 or lower than 5. pH 7.4 was selected as the optimum pH, which was conducive to the sensing of metal ions in real samples and bioimaging.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent sensing of Cr(VI) with ACDs
ce:section.ce:para: The ACDs synthesized were used to test the Cr(VI) content. The influence of acidity on the fluorescence intensities of Cr(VI)-ACDs mixture was tested at first. The fluorescence intensity of ACDs was measured at different pH in the absence (F0) and presence of Cr(VI) (F) at 430 nm, respectively. As displayed in Fig. S5A, fluorescence intensity ratios (F/F0) under alkaline condition were higher than acidic condition. Combined the conclusion of Fig. S4C, pH 7.4 was chosen as the optimum pH condition for the following studies. Furthermore, the response time of ACDs by Cr(VI) was investigated. Fluorescence intensity of the ACDs could be quickly quenched in 10 s (see Fig. S5B), then the F/F0 remained constant in the range of 10 s–120 s. This result indicated that the response of ACDs after the addition of Cr(VI) was very fast.

The fluorescence titration of ACDs with different concentrations of Cr(VI) was performed, and the emission spectra were displayed in Fig. 5 A. It can be seen that the fluorescence of ACDs decreased greatly and gradually as the concentration of Cr(VI) ions increased from 0.5 to 700 μmol/L. We gathered that Cr(VI) bound with the nitrogen atoms of ACDs to formed complex, thus the microenvironment of CDs changed. The quenching can be expressed by Stern−Volmer equation
F 0 / F = 1 + K S V
[ Q ]
, where K
SV was the quenching efficiency constant and [Q] was the concentration of the quencher [38]. Stern−Volmer (SV) plot of Fig. 5B showed the relationship of F0/F data of ACDs and the concentration of Cr(VI). The K
SV was calculated to be 1.002 × 104 L/mol with a correlation coefficient R 2 of 0.9988. The linearity of F/F
0 data versus Cr(VI) concentration was in the range of 0.5–100 μmol/L (i.e., 26.00–5200 μg/L). The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 34 nmol/L based on three times the standard deviation rule [39]. This result was lower than permitted maximum concentration of Cr(VI) level of integrated waste water discharge (50 μg/L, 0.96 μmol/L) based on the World Health Organization (WHO) Standard, indicating the potential application of ACDs in Cr(VI) detection.

Generally speaking, inner filter effect (IFE) requires the sufficient overlaping of the absorption band of an absorber with the excitation and/or emission bands of a fluorophore [40]. As for ACDs, the optimum excitation and emission wavelengths were 350 nm and 430 nm respectively. As shown in Fig. 5C, the UV–vis spectrum of Cr(VI) showed two strong absorption bands located at approximately 255 and 350 nm, and a weak band at around 430 nm which had adequate spectral shield with ACDs. Other ions had no absorption peak around 350 nm and/or 430 nm expect for Fe3+

(absorption band located at approximately 300 nm). Therefore, IFE was considered to be the main possible mechanism for fluorescence quenching of ACDs caused by Cr(VI). In addition, the effect of Cr(VI) on the lifetimes of ACDs was measured and the decay curves were showed in Fig. 5D. Fitted the curves by a double-exponential function, the lifetimes of ACDs were composed of τ1 and τ2, and the relevant data were shown in Table 3S. The average lifetime values of ACDs without and with Cr(VI) were 6.46 and 6.44 ns, respectively, which indicated that there was no significant electron-transfer in the fluorescence quenching process.

To study the possibility of the sensing system for Cr(VI), some common environmentally relevant ions were selected. The fluorescence intensities of the ACDs in the presence of various ions (including Na+, K+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Ba2+, Cu2+, Co2+, Mn2+, Al3+, Fe3+, Cr3+, I−, Br−, NO2 −, S2−, Cr2O7 2−) were carefully examined, as shown in Fig. 6 A. It was found that the fluorescence intensity of ACDs just changed slightly in the presence of these ions at high concentrations (0.33 mmol/L) except Cr(VI) and Fe3+, showing a high selectivity. Moreover, as shown in Fig. 6B, even in the presence of high level of interfering ions (5 equiv. of above-mentioned ions, concentrations were 1.65 mmol/L), the fluorescence quenching induced by Cr(VI) still remained steady, which further proved the anti-interference capability of this method. Considering the interference of Fe3+, NaF solution could be chosen to remove Fe3+ because excessive Na+ and F− ions would not affect the fluorescence of ACDs [41]. By comparison with the previously reported results (Table 1 ), this method exhibited comparable sensitivity, selectivity and rapidity.

In order to investigate the practicability of the present method, three kinds of water samples, tap water of our chemical laboratory (sample 1), pond water in the Science Building (sample 2) and LingDe lake (sample 3) from Shanxi University, were selected to perform Cr(VI) detection. In order to remove the suspended particles, the water samples were filtered through a 0.22 μm microporous membrane and their flurescence intensity was measured directly as above mentioned. The original contents of Cr(VI) in the three kinds of samples were determined to be 27.56 μg/L(0.53 μmol/L), 45.24 μg/L(0.87 μmol/L), 63.96 μg/L(1.23 μmol/L), respectively. The detection recoveries were in the range of 99.70%–108.4% after adding a given amount of Cr(VI), and the relative standard deviation (RSD) were in the range from 1.13% to 1.85% (see Table 2 ). Hence, this result indicated the proposed method had been successfully applied to the trace detection of Cr(VI) in real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Toxicity of the ACDs and bioimaging
ce:section.ce:para: The cytotoxicity of ACDs was measured by the MTT assay in the B16F10 cells. The results were shown in Fig. S6. It should be noted that the concentration of the ACDs was higher than 0.8 mg/mL. Under this condition, more than 90% of the cells were alive and the high viability indicated low cytotoxicity of CDs. In addition, the laser scanning confocal microscopy images (Fig. 7 ) showed that morphologies of B16F10 cells were not changed after incubation, further demonstrating the low cytotoxicity. Moreover, ACDs were internalized into the cell membrane and the cytoplasmic area of the cells possibly by endocytosis mechanism only within 5 min [24] (Fig. S7), and the blue emission in cells remained for 8 h (Fig. S8) when stimulated by 405 nm lasers. The above results demonstrated that the ACDs possessed great potentials in cellular labeling and imaging applications.

OCDs were found to have good solubility in a variety of organic solvents such as DMSO, DMF, acetonitrile, methanol, alcohol, acetone, THF, ethanol, and dichloromethane. All dispersions displayed strong blue fluorescence under 365 nm ultraviolet irradiation as exhibited in insert photograph of Fig. S9. Their fluorescence emission spectra were displayed in Fig. S9. It can be seen from the figure that the solvent affected the optical character of OCDs on emission intensity. The PL intensities of OCDs dissolved in ethanol and THF were the highest. No obvious wavelength shift was observed when the polarity of the solvents changing.

To make full use of OCDs, poly(methyl methacrylate) (PMMA) was selected to fabricate OCDs/polymer composites for the application of potential solid lighting devices [6]. Fig. S10 exhibited the optical and ﬂuorescent images of OCDs/PMMA composite films. When the content of OCDs increased, the fluorescence intensity increased gradually accompanied by a red shift of the emission wavelength. The emission spectra of composites were same as the spectra of OCDs dissolved in dichloromethane, which further confirmed that OCDs were well dispersed into the PMMA. In brief, OCDs could be well dispersed in common organic solvents and organic-soluble polymers, which was greatly advantageous to fabricate photovoltaic materials.

In summary, a simple and convenient one-pot hydrothermal method to synthesis two kinds of CDs was demonstrated. The obtained ACDs and OCDs can be separated and purified easily by their solubility difference. ACDs could serve as a satisfactory sensor for the determination of Cr (VI) in real water samples. In addition, confocal fluorescence imaging of B16F10 cells indicated that ACDs were promising fluorescence label candidates for bioimaging applications with low cytotoxicity. Moreover, the blue emissive OCDs/PMMA composite films demonstrated the potential application in solid lighting devices. This proposed method was novel, simple and had perfect practicability. However, the fluorescence quantum yield of our ACDs and the fluorescence property of OCDs synthesized are still needed to be improved. In addition, like most CDs reported, our ACDs also labeled the cells without reaching the nuclei. They are hoped to be specially functionalized in future work to label cells more efficiently and specifically.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We express our deep thanks to the support of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21575084) and Natural Science Foundation of Shanxi Province (No. 201701D121021). We also acknowledged the help from the Scientific Instrument Center at Shanxi University.

ce:section.ce:para:
